trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 2 && mkdir -p /tmp/test_data && cp /data/val.pt /tmp/test_data/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/test_data --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output /tmp/test_pred.json
trajectory.description: Observation: Starting from scratch on CIFAR-100-LT. Need to establish a strong pretrained baseline quickly and validate the full pipeline (train + predict + eval) in the first iteration. Analysis: Using a pretrained ConvNeXt-Tiny (28M, pre-downloaded) with 224x224 resize, class-balanced CE loss, and RandAugment should give a solid baseline. Decision: Train for 2 epochs to measure iteration time and get a score, then submit eval immediately. Also test predict.py locally with val.pt renamed to test.pt to ensure offline loading works.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 15
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 1 scored 1% balanced_accuracy (random chance). Training loss barely decreased (6.50→6.45) and training accuracy was also 1%. Analysis: The extreme class reweighting (beta=0.9999 effective-number weights with ~100:1 ratio) combined with label smoothing likely caused gradient instability and collapsed predictions when training for only 2 epochs. The model had no chance to learn a balanced classifier. Additionally, metrics.jsonl was not written, so training curves were empty. Decision: Remove aggressive class weighting and label smoothing for now. Train with standard CrossEntropyLoss for 15 epochs to let the pretrained ConvNeXt head learn properly and establish a real baseline. Also fix metrics logging. After this baseline, I can add post-hoc logit adjustment or deferred rebalancing to boost balanced accuracy further.

trajectory.command: mkdir -p /tmp/smoke_test && cp /data/val.pt /tmp/smoke_test/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/smoke_test --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output /tmp/smoke_test/out.json && python -c "import json; d=json.load(open('/tmp/smoke_test/out.json')); print('ok, preds:', len(d['predictions']), 'first 5:', d['predictions'][:5])"
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 2 finished training 15 epochs with standard CE loss. The validation accuracy curve rose to ~64%. However, no eval was submitted yet, so I don't know the balanced_accuracy (primary metric). Analysis: I need the baseline score to guide further improvements. The predict.py script looks correct, but I should verify it runs locally before submitting. Decision: Run a quick local smoke-test of predict.py using val.pt as fake test.pt, then submit the existing checkpoint for evaluation. Based on the balanced_accuracy returned, I'll decide whether to add class-balanced loss, logit adjustment, deferred rebalancing, or train longer.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 eval gave 0.6517 balanced_accuracy with standard CE on ConvNeXt-Tiny (15 epochs). Many-shot accuracy is high (0.89) but few-shot is low (0.37), typical of long-tailed training without rebalancing. Analysis: Post-hoc logit adjustment is a zero-training-cost method that corrects classifier bias using training priors. Since the test set is balanced, subtracting log(prior) from logits should boost tail-class recall and improve balanced accuracy significantly. Decision: 1) Write tune_tau.py to grid-search the temperature tau for logit adjustment on the validation set using the existing checkpoint. 2) Update predict.py to support dict checkpoints containing a pre-computed logit_adj vector. 3) Also update train.py with WeightedRandomSampler, layer-wise LR decay, and logit-adjusted validation for future longer training runs. 4) Run tuning + local smoke-test, then submit eval with the adjusted checkpoint.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 40 --batch_size 128 --lr 1e-3 --weight_decay 0.02 --backbone_lr_decay 0.1 --drop_path 0.1 --mixup_alpha 0.2 --label_smoothing 0.1
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4 achieved 0.7275 balanced_accuracy via post-hoc logit adjustment (tau=1.75) on the 15-epoch standard-CE baseline. The training code in workspace was updated but not yet executed. Analysis: The base model was only trained for 15 epochs without balanced sampling. Training longer with balanced sampling (WeightedRandomSampler), mixup, label smoothing, and layer-wise LR decay should learn better balanced representations, potentially pushing balanced accuracy well above 0.75 directly. Decision: Overwrite train.py with a complete 40-epoch training script using balanced sampling + mixup + label smoothing. Update model.py to accept drop_path_rate for regularization. Update predict.py with simple horizontal-flip TTA. Train for 40 epochs (~12 min expected), then smoke-test predict.py and submit eval.

Validation accuracy is ~67% but training accuracy fluctuated wildly (expected with balanced sampling + mixup). I need the balanced_accuracy of this new model to compare against the current best 0.7275. Analysis: Post-hoc logit adjustment on the previous 15-epoch standard-CE model gave strong gains. If the new model learned better features, combining it with logit adjustment should push the score higher. I must ensure tune_tau.py handles whatever checkpoint format train.py saved (raw state_dict or dict), then tune tau and submit eval. Decision: Update tune_tau.py to robustly load both checkpoint formats and expand tau search to [0.5, 2.5]. Run tuning, smoke-test predict.py, and submit eval with best_adj.pt.

trajectory.description: Observation: The 40-epoch balanced-sampling model scored 0.7182, which is worse than the 15-epoch natural-sampling model (0.7275). The training curves showed extremely noisy train_acc (0.4-0.6) suggesting mixup + balanced sampling caused underfitting. Analysis: Natural sampling with post-hoc logit adjustment is the stronger paradigm here. The 15-epoch model was clearly undertrained (val_acc ~64% and rising), so extending training duration with the simpler natural-sampling recipe should learn richer representations and yield higher balanced accuracy after logit adjustment. Decision: Rewrite train.py for 50 epochs with natural sampling, standard CE, cosine annealing, no mixup/label smoothing, and light regularization. After training, run tune_tau.py and submit eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 20 --drop_path 0.1 --la_tau 1.0 && mkdir -p /tmp/fake_test && cp /data/val.pt /tmp/fake_test/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/fake_test --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output /tmp/fake_test/out.json && head -c 200 /tmp/fake_test/out.json
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 7's 50-epoch natural-sampling model severely overfitted (train_acc ~100%, best val_bacc only 0.633) and its post-hoc logit adjustment scored 0.706, below the best 0.7275 from iteration 4. The smoke test also failed because predict.py looked for /data/test.pt which does not exist in the training container. Analysis: Standard CE with natural sampling quickly overfits to head classes; longer training hurts balanced accuracy. Instead of repeating the 15-epoch standard recipe, I will directly bake rebalancing into training with the Logit-Adjusted Loss (LA loss: CE on logits - log(priors))). This removes the prior bias during training so the model learns balanced representations naturally, allowing longer training without collapse. At test time on balanced data, raw logits should be used directly. Decision: Rewrite train.py with LA loss, 20 epochs, drop_path=0.1, and cosine LR. Run training, perform a local smoke test with a temporary fake test.pt, and submit eval immediately to measure the new baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 8's LA-loss training scored only 0.5729, far below the best 0.7275 from iteration 4. The successful recipe was 15 epochs of natural-sampled standard CE with ConvNeXt-Tiny + post-hoc logit adjustment. Longer training without strong regularization causes severe overfitting to head classes, degrading tail-class features and hurting adjusted balanced accuracy. Analysis: To beat 0.7275, I should return to the proven standard-CE recipe but add regularization (drop_path=0.1, layer-wise LR decay) to learn richer, less overfit features over 20 epochs. Then apply post-hoc logit adjustment via tune_tau.py. This keeps the strong head-class learning of natural sampling while mitigating overfitting, hopefully improving the base model enough to exceed 0.7275 after adjustment. Decision: Rewrite train.py with standard CE, natural sampling, layer-wise LR decay (head LR=1e-3, backbone LR=1e-4), drop_path=0.1, 20 epochs. After training, run tune_tau.py, smoke-test predict.py, and submit eval.

trajectory.command: python retrain_head.py && python tune_tau_crt.py && mkdir -p /tmp/fake_test && cp /data/val.pt /tmp/fake_test/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/fake_test --checkpoint checkpoints/crt_adj.pt --output /tmp/fake_test/out.json && echo 'Smoke test OK' && cat /tmp/fake_test/out.json | head -c 200
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 achieved a new best balanced_accuracy of 0.8514 (up from 0.7275) using natural-sampled standard CE training with layer-wise LR decay and drop_path=0.1 for 20 epochs, followed by post-hoc logit adjustment (tau=1.5). The training curves show severe overfitting (train_acc ~99% vs val_acc ~77%), which means the classifier head is heavily biased toward head classes despite good learned features. Analysis: Decoupled training (classifier retraining, cRT) is a proven long-tailed learning strategy: freeze the well-learned backbone and retrain only the classifier head with balanced sampling. This directly removes head-class bias without destroying the representation. It is also extremely fast (~30-60s) since only ~77K head parameters are updated. Decision: Implement classifier retraining (cRT) on the existing best.pt (iter 9) for 10 epochs using WeightedRandomSampler and head-only AdamW. After retraining, quickly tune tau on the new checkpoint and submit eval. I will also smoke-test predict.py locally before submitting.

trajectory.description: Observation: The best balanced_accuracy so far is 0.8514 from iteration 9 (ConvNeXt-Tiny, 20 epochs natural CE + post-hoc logit adjustment tau=1.5). Iteration 10's classifier retraining dropped to 0.8071, confirming that the raw natural-sampling features plus logit adjustment are the strongest single-model recipe. The training curves show severe overfitting (train_acc ~99% vs val_acc ~77%), meaning the base model has room to improve with stronger regularization. Analysis: To beat 0.8514, I should improve the base ConvNeXt model's generalization. Adding an exponential moving average (EMA) of weights produces a more stable model that often generalizes better. Switching from RandAugment to TrivialAugmentWide and training for 30 epochs should learn richer representations while keeping tail-class features intact. Post-hoc logit adjustment on this stronger base should yield higher balanced accuracy. Decision: Rewrite train.py with EMA (decay=0.999), TrivialAugmentWide, and 30 epochs. Backup the current best checkpoints. Retrain, tune tau, smoke-test predict.py, and submit eval.

trajectory.command: python train.py --epochs 20 --drop_path 0.15 --mixup 0.2 --label_smoothing 0.1 --output_name mixup.pt --seed 123 && python ensemble_tune.py --ckpt1 checkpoints/best_iter9.pt --ckpt2 checkpoints/mixup.pt --output checkpoints/ens_adj.pt && mkdir -p /tmp/fake_test && cp /data/val.pt /tmp/fake_test/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path /tmp/fake_test --checkpoint checkpoints/ens_adj.pt --output /tmp/fake_test/out.json && echo "Smoke test OK"
trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 11 scored 0.7861, well below the best 0.8514 from iteration 9. The best recipe is clearly iteration 9's ConvNeXt-Tiny with natural CE + drop_path=0.1 + layer-wise LR for 20 epochs, followed by post-hoc logit adjustment. Analysis: Rather than chasing a radically different training recipe, the most reliable way to beat 0.8514 is to create a diverse ensemble. I will keep the proven iteration 9 checkpoint (best_iter9.pt) and train a second ConvNeXt-Tiny model with a slightly different regularization stack (Mixup α=0.2, label smoothing=0.1, drop_path=0.15, TrivialAugmentWide, seed=123) to encourage diversity. After training, I'll grid-search the ensemble weight and logit-adjustment temperature on the validation set, save a joint ensemble checkpoint, and update predict.py to load it. Decision: Write new train.py with Mixup/label smoothing, new ensemble_tune.py, and ensemble-capable predict.py. Run training, ensemble tuning, smoke test, and submit eval in one iteration.

trajectory.command: python train.py --arch vit_clip --epochs 20 --drop_path 0.1 --lr 1e-3 --backbone_lr_decay 0.1 --output_name vit_clip.pt --seed 123 && python tune_tau.py --checkpoint checkpoints/vit_clip.pt --output checkpoints/vit_clip_adj.pt && cp /data/val.pt /data/test.pt && python predict.py --data_path /data --checkpoint checkpoints/vit_clip_adj.pt --output /tmp/smoke.json && rm /data/test.pt && echo "Smoke test passed"

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 12 failed immediately with a RuntimeError because the model weights were not moved to CUDA before the forward pass. The current best score is 0.8514 from iteration 9 (ConvNeXt-Tiny, 20 epochs natural CE + post-hoc logit adjustment). Analysis: To beat 0.8514, I will switch to the much stronger CLIP ViT-B/16 pretrained model (86M params, available offline via timm). It provides significantly richer visual representations than ConvNeXt-Tiny. I will train it with the same proven recipe as iteration 9 (natural sampling, standard CE, layer-wise LR decay, drop_path=0.1, RandAugment, 20 epochs), but using the model's native preprocessing mean/std extracted from timm's default_cfg so the backbone sees correctly normalized inputs. I will also fix the CUDA bug by explicitly calling model.to(device) and add gradient clipping for training stability. After training, I will tune tau with the updated tune_tau.py and run a smoke test before submitting eval. If CLIP ViT scores >0.85, I can ensemble it with the iteration 9 ConvNeXt in a follow-up iteration for even higher balanced accuracy.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 13's CLIP ViT experiment failed catastrophically (val_acc ~19%, bacc ~23%). The proven best remains iteration 9's ConvNeXt-Tiny with natural CE + layer-wise LR + drop_path=0.1 for 20 epochs, followed by post-hoc logit adjustment (tau=1.5), scoring 0.8514. Analysis: Instead of chasing a weaker architecture, I should improve the strong ConvNeXt model via a model soup: train a second ConvNeXt with the exact same hyperparameters but a different seed (123), then grid-search the interpolation weight (alpha) that maximizes validation balanced accuracy when merging the two state dicts. Averaging weights of two fine-tuned models often finds a broader minimum that generalizes better. After merging, I'll tune tau and evaluate. Decision: 1) Rewrite train.py cleanly with the proven recipe and seed argument. 2) Write tune_soup.py to search alpha ∈ {0.3,0.4,0.5,0.6,0.7} and save the best merged checkpoint. 3) Train seed=123 model, run soup tuning, run tau tuning, smoke-test predict.py, and submit eval—all in one chained command.

trajectory.description: Observation: The current best balanced_accuracy is 0.8514 from iteration 9 (ConvNeXt-Tiny, seed 42). Iteration 14 trained a second ConvNeXt with seed 123 (convnext_s2.pt) and created a weight-soup that scored 0.8462, slightly below the best. A logit-level ensemble of the two raw ConvNeXt models should outperform the weight-soup because it preserves model diversity and allows per-sample arbitration. This requires zero training time. Analysis: Since both models share the same preprocessing, I can average their logits on the validation set, grid-search the interpolation weight and post-hoc logit-adjustment temperature, and save a joint ensemble checkpoint. I must update predict.py to load ensemble checkpoints containing multiple model state dicts. Decision: Update ensemble_tune.py and predict.py for robust multi-model ensemble support, then immediately run ensemble tuning on best_iter9.pt + convnext_s2.pt, smoke-test, and submit eval. If this doesn't beat 0.8514, the next iteration will train a ResNet50 for architectural diversity.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 15 successfully created an ensemble checkpoint (ensemble_cnext.pt) combining best_iter9.pt and convnext_s2.pt with weights 0.8/0.2 and logit adjustment tau=1.75, achieving val balanced accuracy 0.8560 (vs previous best test 0.8514). However, the smoke test failed because predict.py tried to load /data/test.pt which doesn't exist in the training container, causing the command to exit 1 and skipping eval submission. Analysis: The ensemble checkpoint is ready and predict.py logic is correct; the failure was solely due to the missing local test file. I need to perform a local smoke test using a copied val.pt as fake test.pt in a writable directory, then submit the ensemble for evaluation. Decision: Run a quick local smoke test with a fake test.pt copy to verify predict.py loads the ensemble checkpoint correctly, then immediately submit eval for checkpoints/ensemble_cnext.pt.

trajectory.description: Observation: The current best test balanced_accuracy is 0.8539 from iteration 16 (ensemble_fine.pt: 95% ConvNeXt seed42 + 5% ConvNeXt seed123, tau=1.70). Iteration 19's 3-way ensemble with ResNet50 did not improve (also 0.8539), confirming that the third model adds no value. Analysis: Since training new models has not beaten the existing strong ConvNeXt base, the most efficient way to push the score higher is stronger test-time augmentation (TTA). Currently predict.py only averages original + horizontal-flip logits (2 views). Multi-scale TTA (e.g., 224/240/256 + flips = 6 views) is a standard, zero-training-cost technique that often improves balanced accuracy by smoothing predictions. To maximize benefit, I will re-tune the ensemble weight and logit-adjustment temperature on the validation set using the exact same multi-scale TTA pipeline that will be used at test time. This ensures the hyperparameters are optimal for the true inference setup. Decision: Write a new tuning script (tune_ensemble_tta.py) and update predict.py to support 6-view multi-scale TTA. Run tuning on best_iter9.pt + convnext_s2.pt, save the new ensemble checkpoint, perform a local smoke test, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The current best test balanced_accuracy is 0.8539 from iteration 16 (ensemble_fine.pt), achieved with flip-TTA predict.py. Iteration 20 switched predict.py to multi-scale TTA and the score dropped to 0.8515, suggesting multi-scale TTA hurts on this small-image dataset. Analysis: I need to (1) revert predict.py to flip-only TTA to match the best result's inference pipeline, and (2) train a diverse third model to improve the ensemble. The best single-model recipe (iter9) uses natural-sampled CE with ConvNeXt-Tiny. To create a diverse ensemble member, I will train another ConvNeXt-Tiny from scratch using Focal Loss (gamma=1.0), which down-weights easy head-class examples and focuses on hard tail-class examples, learning different representations. Then I'll ensemble it with best_iter9.pt via TTA-aware grid search. Decision: Rewrite predict.py with flip-only TTA, rewrite train.py to support Focal Loss, rewrite model.py for robustness, then train a focal-loss model (seed=456), run ensemble tuning, smoke-test, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The current best test balanced_accuracy is 0.8539 from iteration 16 (ensemble_fine.pt). Training new models (ResNet50, Focal Loss ConvNeXt) has failed to improve the ensemble. Analysis: The best models suffer from classifier weight bias typical of long-tailed natural-sampling training. Tau-normalization (dividing each class logit by ||w_c||^tau) is a zero-cost post-hoc method from the decoupling literature that directly corrects classifier weight magnitudes, often improving balanced accuracy significantly. I will apply tau-normalization to the two existing ConvNeXt checkpoints (best_iter9.pt and convnext_s2.pt), grid-search tau_norm and logit-adjustment temperature on the validation set, then re-run the TTA-aware ensemble search on the normalized models. This requires no training and could push the score above 0.8539. Decision: Write tune_tau_norm.py to bake tau-normalization into checkpoint state dicts, then chain tuning for both models, ensemble grid search, smoke test, and eval submission.

trajectory.description: Observation: The current best test balanced_accuracy is 0.8539 from iteration 16 (ensemble_fine.pt). Training additional ConvNeXt backbones with different seeds/losses has not improved the ensemble beyond this. Analysis: The best backbone is still the iteration 9 ConvNeXt-Tiny trained with natural sampling. Its classifier head is heavily biased toward head classes. Classifier retraining (cRT) on frozen features should remove this bias, but the previous cRT attempt (iter 10) was likely under-trained or unstable. By caching backbone features (~10k vectors) and training a new linear head for 100 epochs with balanced sampling in seconds, we can do cRT properly. We will then ensemble this cRT model with the original best_iter9.pt via TTA-aware logit-adjustment grid search. This is fast (<5 min) and could push the score above 0.8539. Decision: Write retrain_head.py to extract features from best_iter9.pt, retrain the head with balanced sampling, tune tau, save the model, then immediately run ensemble fine-tuning against best_iter9.pt, smoke-test, and submit eval.

Decision: Write mega_ensemble.py to precompute flip-TTA validation logits for all 5 checkpoints, then exhaustively search pairwise (weight step 0.05, tau step 0.05) and triple (weight step 0.1, tau step 0.1) combinations, saving the best ensemble as mega_best.pt. Then smoke-test predict.py and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The best test balanced_accuracy remains 0.8539 from iteration 16 (ensemble_fine.pt). Multiple recent attempts (3-way ensembles, tau-normalization, cRT, focal loss, new seeds) have failed to improve. Analysis: The base ConvNeXt-Tiny (best_iter9.pt) is the strongest anchor. Instead of training a new backbone from scratch, I will fine-tune this existing checkpoint with stronger regularization (MixUp α=0.2, RandomErasing p=0.25, RandAugment magnitude 15, RandomResizedCrop) and a lower learning rate (backbone 5e-5, head 5e-4) for 15 epochs. This should nudge the model to a broader minimum with different error patterns, especially for tail classes, while preserving the strong head-class features. After fine-tuning, I will immediately run a TTA-aware ensemble grid search between the original best_iter9.pt and the new fine-tuned model, then submit the resulting ensemble. This is fast (~6-7 min total) and low-risk because the ensemble can down-weight the new model if it underperforms.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 26 achieved a new best test balanced_accuracy of 0.8544 (up from 0.8539) by fine-tuning best_iter9.pt with stronger regularization, then ensembling it 50/50 with the original best_iter9.pt (tau=1.55). The fine-tuned model alone only had val bacc 0.7690, but the ensemble worked well due to diversity. Time remaining: ~69 minutes, 4 iterations. Analysis: Training new backbones has hit diminishing returns. The fastest path to improvement is to leverage the newly created ft.pt checkpoint and search for better ensemble combinations that include it, without any additional training. I already have logits cached for several other checkpoints. I just need to compute logits for ft.pt and run a comprehensive 2-way/3-way/4-way ensemble grid search over all combinations involving ft.pt. Decision: Write tune_mega_v2.py that computes TTA logits for ft.pt, then exhaustively searches ensembles with best_iter9.pt, ft.pt, and other available models (convnext_s2, convnext_s3, resnet50, crt_v2) using a fine grid. Save the best ensemble and submit eval.

Analysis: Since the new ensemble shows the highest validation score seen so far and requires zero training time, it is the most promising candidate to beat the current test best. I should verify predict.py can load it locally, then submit it for evaluation immediately. Decision: Run a quick smoke test with mega_best_v2.pt using a local fake test copy, then submit eval. If it underperforms, I still have ~50 minutes and 2 iterations to attempt one more training run.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 28 failed during a smoke test for mega_best_v2.pt because the checkpoint contained numpy scalar types incompatible with torch.load's default weights_only=True. The best test balanced_accuracy remains 0.8544 from iteration 26 (ensemble_ft.pt). Analysis: Instead of debugging the corrupted checkpoint, I can leverage the fact that all 6 trained models have cached flip-TTA validation logits available. A fast, exhaustive ensemble search over all pairs, triples, and selected 4-way combinations using these precomputed logits requires no neural network forward passes and will complete in under a minute. By ensuring the saved ensemble checkpoint uses only native Python floats and torch Tensors (no numpy types), predict.py will load it successfully. Decision: Write final_ensemble.py to load all cached logits, perform an exhaustive grid search over ensemble weights and logit-adjustment temperature, save the best clean ensemble checkpoint, run a smoke test, and immediately submit it for evaluation.
